---
title: "NZ Migration: What the Data Shows"
subtitle: "Monthly migration trends tracked with Statistics NZ data — built by [Autonomous Econ](https://autonomousecon.substack.com)"
---

## Kiwis are leaving faster than they return

Net NZ citizen migration peaked at +24,572 in the 12 months to October 2020 while the border was closed and departures collapsed. By March 2026 it stood at -36,538. Over the same 12 months, non-NZ citizens (including Australians) recorded a net +60,787 — down from the post-reopening peak of +176,489 in October 2023.

```{=html}
{{< include data/kiwi-exodus_main.html >}}
```

<p class="chart-subtitle">Rolling 12-month sum.</p>
<p class="data-source">Source: Statistics NZ ITM552301</p>

## The post-COVID surge was overwhelmingly young

Net migration is dominated by people in their 20s and 30s throughout the series. The post-2022 cycle saw those cohorts reach levels far above anything in the pre-COVID baseline before the recent slowdown.

```{=html}
{{< include data/kiwi-exodus_age_net.html >}}
```

<p class="chart-subtitle">Rolling 12-month net migration (arrivals minus departures), all citizenships.</p>
<p class="data-source">Source: Statistics NZ ITM552101</p>

## India now supplies one in four non-NZ arrivals

The composition of net migration has shifted markedly since 2022. India and China now dominate inflows, while the "Other" category has compressed as migration becomes more concentrated.

```{=html}
{{< include data/india-surge_net_area.html >}}
```

<p class="chart-subtitle">Rolling 12-month net migration (arrivals minus departures), non-NZ citizens. China, India, Philippines, Sri Lanka, UK, Fiji plus Other.</p>
<p class="data-source">Source: Statistics NZ ITM552301</p>

The share chart shows each country's arrivals as a proportion of all non-NZ arrivals on a 12-month rolling average.

```{=html}
{{< include data/india-surge_share.html >}}
```

<p class="chart-subtitle">Rolling 12-month average share of non-NZ arrivals.</p>
<p class="data-source">Source: Statistics NZ ITM552301</p>

## Work visas, not students, powered the surge

The arrivals chart shows the surge in visa mix terms — what proportion of people crossing the border held each visa type.

```{=html}
{{< include data/visa-shift_overall.html >}}
```

<p class="chart-subtitle">Monthly arrivals by visa type.</p>
<p class="data-source">Source: Statistics NZ ITM552201</p>

Approved work visa decisions peaked sharply in late 2022 as the AEWV replaced Essential Skills. Skilled Work approvals have since moderated following the April 2024 reforms, while Working Holiday and Relationship visas have recovered from their COVID-era lows.

```{=html}
{{< include data/visa-shift_w1_decisions.html >}}
```

<p class="chart-subtitle">W1 Work Decisions, approved decisions by application substream. Rolling 12-month sum.</p>
<p class="data-source">Source: MBIE Migration Data Explorer</p>

```{=html}
{{< include data/visa-shift_india_clpr.html >}}
```

<p class="chart-subtitle">Arrivals where Country of Last Permanent Residence = India.</p>
<p class="data-source">Source: Statistics NZ ITM553001</p>

```{=html}
{{< include data/visa-shift_china_clpr.html >}}
```

<p class="chart-subtitle">Arrivals where Country of Last Permanent Residence = China.</p>
<p class="data-source">Source: Statistics NZ ITM553001</p>

## Lower-skill work visa share reaches 49% for some nationalities

India's work visa intake has shifted from high-skill toward lower-skill roles since the AEWV launched in mid-2022.

```{=html}
{{< include data/india-surge_skill_shift.html >}}
```

<p class="chart-subtitle">W3 Work Occupations, approved work visas by nationality and ANZSCO skill level (12-month periods ended May 2017–2026).</p>
<p class="data-source">Source: MBIE Migration Data Explorer</p>
<p class="data-source">Note: W3 covers records where INZ captured an occupation (~60% of approved work visa decisions). Open work right holders (Working Holiday, Relationship) are largely excluded. Roles with no recorded skill level are excluded from proportions.</p>

India's lower-skill share (36%) is above countries like the UK (16%) and China (11%) — but Vietnam (49%) and Fiji (48%) rank higher. The pattern is not India-specific.

```{=html}
{{< include data/india-surge_country_skill.html >}}
```

<p class="chart-subtitle">W3 Work Occupations, year ended May 2026. Top 10 source countries by approved work visa volume.</p>
<p class="data-source">Source: MBIE Migration Data Explorer</p>
<p class="data-source">Note: W3 covers records where INZ captured an occupation (~60% of approved work visa decisions). Open work right holders (Working Holiday, Relationship) are largely excluded. Roles with no recorded skill level are excluded from proportions.</p>

## Auckland absorbs most arrivals; Queenstown punches above its weight

International migration is concentrated in a handful of Territorial Authorities. Auckland and Hamilton lead by absolute numbers, but smaller TAs like Queenstown-Lakes rank higher on a per-capita basis. The map shows cumulative net international migration per 1,000 residents over the three years ended June 2025 — teal indicates net inflow, red indicates net outflow.

```{=html}
{{< include data/where-they-land_map.html >}}
```

<p class="chart-subtitle">Net international migration = sum of annual net flows, years ended June 2023–2025. Rate per 1,000 = cumulative net ÷ 2025 population × 1,000.</p>
<p class="data-source">Source: Stats NZ, Subnational population estimates at 30 June 2025</p>
<p class="data-source">Note: Data is provisional (2025 boundaries).</p>

The top 5 TAs by per-capita inflow, and a closer look at the 21 Auckland local board areas.

```{=html}
{{< include data/where-they-land_top_inflow.html >}}
```

<p class="data-source">Source: Stats NZ, Subnational population estimates at 30 June 2025</p>
<p class="data-source">Note: Data is provisional (2025 boundaries).</p>

```{=html}
{{< include data/where-they-land_auckland_map.html >}}
```

<p class="chart-subtitle">Boundaries: Stats NZ Territorial Authority Local Board 2025 (Clipped).</p>
<p class="data-source">Source: Stats NZ, Subnational population estimates at 30 June 2025</p>
<p class="data-source">Note: Data is provisional (2025 boundaries).</p>

<p class="data-source">
Built by <a href="https://autonomousecon.substack.com">Autonomous Econ</a> |
<a href="https://github.com/martingeew/nz_migration_streamlit">Source code</a>
</p>
